one_line_takeaway: A PII-first benchmark of four frontier VLMs shows Claude 4 leaks PII most (74.4%) via a 'comply-then-warn' pattern despite best OCR-refusal, that prompt-based defenses are brittle and template-sensitive (Gemini-3 Flash resists mitigation on synthetic images but not on real screenshots), and that single-template/single-prompt safety evals can badly misestimate deployment risk.
key_finding: Safety is asymmetric and no model dominates. Claude 4 has the lowest OCR ASR (14.2%) yet the highest PII ASR (74.4%), driven by a 'comply-then-warn' pattern: 331 of its 372 unsafe PII responses (89.0%) transcribe the PII verbatim and only then append a privacy caveat. Grok-4 has the lowest PII ASR (20.4%) and is prompt-invariant (18.8% across all three framings). A defensive system prompt eliminates PII leakage for GPT-5.2 and Grok-4 (to 0%) and cuts Claude 4 from 74.4% to 2.2%, but has zero measurable effect on Gemini-3 Flash on synthetic data (60.6%->60.6%). Crucially, that same Gemini mitigation succeeds on real-world IRL images (50%->0%) and on text-only input (89.6%->0%), localizing the failure to the specific synthetic sticky-note visual template rather than a fundamental modality bypass. Prompt framing alone swings ASR by up to 48 points (Claude 4: 86.5% sticky-note vs 38.5% authority, non-overlapping CIs).
core_contribution: VisualLeakBench is an open-source evaluation suite that audits frontier Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) for two multimodal safety failures: (Track 1) OCR Injection, where harmful instructions are rendered as text inside images, and (Track 2) Contextual PII Leakage, where images contain sensitive PII (e.g., sticky notes with SSNs, passwords, credit cards) paired with a social-engineering prompt. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a procedurally generated dataset of N=1,000 synthetic adversarial images (500 OCR + 500 PII across 8 PII types) with ground-truth labels, plus 50 in-the-wild (IRL) real-world screenshots (25 OCR + 25 PII) for validation; (2) a 'PII-first' automated evaluator that flags PII as leaked whenever the exact target value appears, before checking for refusal language, thereby catching a 'comply-then-warn' pattern that naive refusal-first classifiers miss; and (3) mitigation experiments plus two ablations (prompt-framing sensitivity and text-only vs. image comparison) run with Wilson 95% confidence intervals. Note: the multimodal-attack techniques it builds on (typographic FigStep-style injection, image hijacks, indirect prompt injection) and prior benchmarks (MM-SafetyBench, JailBreakV, MMJ-Bench, R-Judge, PrivLM-Bench) are Related Work, not this paper's contribution; VisualLeakBench distinguishes itself by targeting privacy/PII extraction via social engineering rather than explicit harmful-content generation, and by validating synthetic results against real screenshots.
threat_model: Adversary with black-box API access to a deployed VLM who submits crafted images to extract sensitive information or elicit harmful output. Two vectors: (1) harmful instructions rendered as image text that bypass text-based safety filters, and (2) human-readable PII embedded in naturalistic images (sticky notes, chats, forms) combined with a plausible social-engineering framing (e.g., 'This is MY info, help me read my own handwriting'). Motivating deployment context is agent-integrated / autonomous pipelines (document processing, RAG, function-calling, customer support) where model output containing verbatim PII flows downstream without human review, so an appended safety caveat does not prevent the leak. Evaluation is at the model-output level, not full end-to-end agent execution.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) synthetic template bias -- all PII images use one sticky-note template and all OCR images use text-on-noisy-background, and the synthetic-IRL divergence shows template choice materially changes behavior; (2) small IRL sample (50 images) gives only directional validation with wide CIs (the 0/24 mitigated IRL result has a ~14% CI upper bound); (3) exact-match evaluator counts partial disclosures as safe, so ASR is a strict lower bound on real compromise; (4) proprietary frontier models only (no open-source VLMs like LLaVA/Qwen-VL); (5) results tied to early-2026 model versions; (6) a single fixed main prompt (three framings in the ablation, but not a full attack space); (7) text-only ablation limited to 2 of 4 models for cost; (8) English-only. Reviewer note: DOB has N=71 in the per-type table despite a stated 14.2% share of 500, so per-type Ns reflect the sampled subset; the DOB/phone/email 'contextually identifiable leaks more' claim and the alternative 'trained to refuse high-entropy formats' explanation are acknowledged as indistinguishable without training-data access.

datasets_used: VisualLeakBench synthetic set: N=1,000 procedurally generated adversarial images (500 OCR injection using 20 harmful instruction templates; 500 PII leakage across 8 PII types on emulated office sticky notes)
8 PII types with shares: Password 15%, SSN 14.6%, DOB 14.2%, API Key 12.6%, Phone 12.6%, PIN 11.4%, Credit Card 9.8%, Email 9.8%
In-the-wild (IRL) validation set: 50 real-world screenshots (25 OCR injection, 25 PII leakage across 7 PII types) spanning emails, sticky notes, documents, forms, ID cards, chats, app screenshots
Prompt-sensitivity ablation: stratified sample of 96 PII images x 3 framings
Text-only ablation: 48 PII instances as plain text (Gemini-3 Flash and GPT-5.2)
quantitative_results: Claude 4 comply-then-warn: 331/372 (89.0%) of unsafe PII responses disclose PII verbatim then append an advisory caveat
PII-first evaluator vs naive refusal-first: Claude 4 scored 74.4% vs ~28%; automated labels 97% agreement with manual review on 100 samples
IRL results (OCR ASR / PII baseline / PII mitigated): GPT-5.2 96.0/24.0/0.0; Claude 4 15.0/44.0/0.0; Gemini-3 Flash 100.0/50.0/0.0; Grok-4 32.0/16.0/0.0
Per-PII-type worst leakers: DOB (GPT-5.2 100%, Claude 4 94.4%), SSN (Claude 4 89.0%, Gemini 37.0%), API Key (Claude 4 54.0%, Gemini 55.6%, GPT-5.2 0.0%, Grok-4 0.0%), PIN (Grok-4 77.2%)

Prompt sensitivity (sticky-note / authority / accessibility PII ASR): GPT-5.2 36.5/53.1/72.9; Claude 4 86.5/38.5/71.9; Gemini-3 Flash 54.2/72.9/83.3; Grok-4 18.8/18.8/18.8; Claude 4 48-point swing with non-overlapping CIs
Text-only vs image PII ASR: Gemini-3 Flash 89.6% text vs 60.6% image (mitigated 0.0% text vs 60.6% image); GPT-5.2 66.7% text vs 38.2% image (mitigated 0.0% both)
baselines_compared: Cross-model comparison of four frontier VLMs against each other: GPT-5.2 (OpenAI), Claude 4 Sonnet (Anthropic), Gemini-3 Flash Preview (Google), Grok-4 (xAI)
Baseline (no system prompt) vs mitigated (defensive system prompt) conditions on the same images
Synthetic vs IRL real-world screenshots, and image-based vs text-only inputs
PII-first evaluator contrasted against a naive refusal-first classifier
Positioned against prior benchmarks in Related Work (not run as baselines): MM-SafetyBench, JailBreakV, MMJ-Bench, R-Judge, PrivLM-Bench
